coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin coordination polymer nanosheets modified with carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive ammonia sensors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dongzhi
Luo, Yuwei
Huang, Zhaodi
Tang, Mingcong
Sun, Jianpeng
Wang, Xiaokang
Wang, Xia
Wang, Yutong
Wu, Wenting
Dai, Fangna
coredata.dc:description: Exposure to ammonia (NH3) is known harmful to health, environment and industrial facilities, hence it is important for the trace detection of NH3. Herein, for the first time, ultrasensitive room temperature NH3 sensors are realized by assembling carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on free-standing ultrathin coordination polymers (CPs) nanosheets (Co[Ni(CN)4]) with an average thickness of ∼2.5 nm, which demonstrate excellent sensitivity (Ra/Rg = 87.7 to 30 ppm), fast gas response speed (∼10 s to 30 ppm), remarkable repeatability, high selectivity, good long-term stability and low limit of theoretical detection (∼8 ppb) toward NH3 gas. The NH3 gas sensor enhancement through incorporation of CQDs provides a simple and environment-friendly strategy for further improving sensor property of CPs nanosheets. This work provides an effective way to construct new electrode materials for high-performance gas sensor devices via the rational design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrathin nanosheet
Coordination polymer
Carbon quantum dots
Ammonia sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs. a) and b) TEM and HRTEM image. c) AFM image. d) Dark-field STEM image and the corresponding EDS mapping.
Characterization of the CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs. a) and b) TEM and HRTEM image. c) HRTEM image of a typical CQDs. d) AFM image. e) Dark-field STEM image and the corresponding EDS mapping.

a) Fluorescence spectrum of a standard solution with a known CQDs concentration (the mass of CQDs are 0.0025 mg, 0.005 mg, 0.02 mg, 0.05 mg, 0.1 mg and 0.15 mg, and the solution was fixed to 2 mL during the experiment). b) Fluorescence spectra of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs loaded with different contents of CQDs (the solution was fixed to 2 mL during the experiment). c) Fitting curve of standard solution and estimated actual load of CQDs (wt.%) deposited on Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs. d) Conductivities of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs with various contents of CQDs (RH = 67 %).
a) Response to 1 ppm NH3 and resistance of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensors with various contents of CQDs (RH = 67 %). b)-h) Physical photo of CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs with various contents of CQDs.

Room temperature gas sensing performances (RH = 67 %) toward varying concentrations of NH3: a) Responses and b) function fitting curves of the CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor. c) Responses and d) function fitting curves of the Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor. e) Repeatability for 0.5, 0.75, 1.0 and 10 ppm NH3. f) Selectivity for 5 ppm of various gas species of the Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensors.
Response-recovery time to various concentration NH3 of the a) CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and c) Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor. Long term stability of the b) CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and d) Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor.
Linear behavior of the response vs both humidity and NH3 concentration for a) Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and b) CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs film.

PXRD patterns: (a) Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and (b) CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs before (black) and after (red) contact with NH3. FT-IR spectra: (c) Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and (d) CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs before (black) and after (pink) contact with NH3. UPS results (the inset is the calculated band alignment): (e) Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and (f) CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs before (black) and after (red) contact with NH3. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs for gas sensor.
Performance of the NH3 sensor in this work compared with previous work.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin coordination polymer nanosheets modified with carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive ammonia sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Control Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Control Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Control Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, Institute of New Energy, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580 China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exposure to ammonia (NH3) is known harmful to health, environment and industrial facilities, hence it is important for the trace detection of NH3. Herein, for the first time, ultrasensitive room temperature NH3 sensors are realized by assembling carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on free-standing ultrathin coordination polymers (CPs) nanosheets (Co[Ni(CN)4]) with an average thickness of ∼2.5 nm, which demonstrate excellent sensitivity (Ra/Rg = 87.7 to 30 ppm), fast gas response speed (∼10 s to 30 ppm), remarkable repeatability, high selectivity, good long-term stability and low limit of theoretical detection (∼8 ppb) toward NH3 gas. The NH3 gas sensor enhancement through incorporation of CQDs provides a simple and environment-friendly strategy for further improving sensor property of CPs nanosheets. This work provides an effective way to construct new electrode materials for high-performance gas sensor devices via the rational design.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of toxic and hazardous gases has attracted considerable attention in recent years due to the increasing human health concerns and environmental pollution. As a toxic gas, ammonia (NH3) is a colorless gas with a pungent odor, which is widely used in chemical industries, fertilizer plants, etc [1–4]. It is also a typical biomarker for the detection of kidney and liver diseases for breath analysis [5–7]. Exposure to excess ammonia (NH3) is known harmful to health, environment and industrial facilities. Therefore, the development of a sensor for NH3 leakage is very important. In the past few decades, NH3 sensors based on graphene, metal oxides, conductive polymers and transition metal sulfides have been widely studied due to low cost, simple operation, high sensitivity and fast response/recovery time [8–12]. However, these sensors typically need to operate at high temperatures, which input uncertain safety issues and high power consumption. Therefore, it is urgent to develop new materials with high-performance detection of NH3 at room temperature.

Coordination polymers (CPs), also known as metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), are a class of crystallized porous materials formed by single metal ions or metal clusters linked by organic ligands with periodic net structure [13–15]. Due to the large surface areas and adjustable pores, CPs have become attractive materials for many applications including of gas storage and separation, energy storage and conversion, catalysis, sensors, biomedicine, etc [16–19]. Even though CPs are promising candidates for gas sensing applications, the traditional bulk CPs materials are difficult to form a compact film to construct electro-device, which is the specific requirement for many applications including gas sensor [20–23]. Furthermore, as most CPs are generally not good electrical conducting materials due to the insulating ligands and poor overlap between p orbitals of ligands and d orbitals of the metal ions [24–26]. Therefore, to overcome the above obstacles, it is needed to explore suitable CPs materials with adjustable film-forming properties and promote conductivity.

Differing from the bulk materials, ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets (NSs) materials, featuring thickness of a few nanometers and large scale lateral size, exhibit unique physical and chemical characteristics [27–30]. Compared to the bulk CPs, more accessible active sites are exposed on the surface of 2D CPs NSs rather than enclosed inside in the pores or channels, which facilitate more interactions between active sites and gas molecules, will improve the gas sensing performance [31,32]. Simultaneously, in order to compensate the insufficient electrical conductivity of CPs materials, compositional adjustment is a quite good choice [33]. Due to the high surface area and rapid electron transfer, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been recognized as a promising candidate for applications in sensing [34,35]. However, the attempt of CQDs/CPs composites for gas sensing has not been reported until now.

Herein, we demonstrate the fabrication of the 2D ultrathin CPs (Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs via a simple bottom-up method. The obtained CPs NSs possess planar square structure (∼50 nm) and ultrathin thickness (∼2.5 nm). Through incorporation of CQDs onto the surface of Co[Ni(CN)]4, the conductivity of CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs have been successfully improved compared with 2D Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs (4.162 × 10-3 vs 2.347 × 10-4), and the NH3 sensor properties of these CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs are obviously improved. The CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs exhibit unparalleled NH3 sensitivity (Ra/Rg = 87.7 vs 80.87 to 30 ppm), fast gas response speed (∼10 s vs 14 s to 30 ppm) and superior cross-selectivity against other interfering gases. Furthermore, the detection limit of gas has been drastically increased (LOD= ∼8 ppb vs ∼ 85 ppb).

Reagents used in the experiment including cobaltous nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 99 %), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, average mol wt 40,000), potassium nickel cyanide (K2[Ni(CN)4]), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, 99 %), methanol (CH3OH, 99 %) and Na2EDTA (C10H14N2Na2O8·2H2O, 99 %) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. and used without further purification.

A two-dimensional nanomaterial, Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs, was prepared by a simple method at room temperature. In this process, the first step was to dissolve Co(NO3)2·6H2O (26 mg) and PVP (100 mg) in 5 mL of CH3OH and stirred to a homogeneous solution. And then K2[Ni(CN)4] (24 mg) and Na3C6H5O7·2H2O (21.5 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of methanol–water mixtures (V:V = 1:1) and stirred to a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the solution was mixed and ultrasound oscillated for 1 min. Then use a vortex oscillator for 5 min and ultrasound oscillate for 1 min to get a completely mixed homogeneous solution. Repeat these steps once after standing solution for 10 min. After standing at room temperature, the solution was found to be cloudy. Subsequently, the Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed more than 5 times with CH3OH. Finally, the obtained Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs was redispersed in 50 mL CH3OH for further use.

A quartz boat filled with Na2EDTA (analytical reagent, 1.6 g) was placed in the center of a quartz tube and calcined in a tube furnace at 250 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min under a N2 atmosphere. The product was ground and dissolved in water (100 mL), and the suspension was ultrasonically treated (300 W, 40 kHz) for 15 min at room temperature, and then centrifuged at a high speed (10000 rpm) for 20 min. The upper brown solution was filtered using a slow-speed quantitative millipore filter to remove the non-fluorescent deposited Na salts. After the filtration process, the solution was dialyzed with a MD34 (3500 Da) dialysis tube for 48 h to remove the remaining salts and small fragments. CQDs powder was obtained by drying the concentrated solution at 60 °C for 24 h. The powder is soluble in water and some organic polar solvents. Finally, the sample was dissolved in deionized water.

NSs. 1 mL, 2.5 mL, 4.5 mL, 10 mL, 15 mL and 30 mL as-synthesized CQDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) was added into 10 mL Co[Ni(CN)4] respectively under stirring, and then the mixed solution was further stirred at room temperature for 3 h. Subsequently, CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed twice with CH3OH. Finally, the obtained CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs was redispersed in 10 mL CH3OH, which was used as the sensing layer for ammonia detection.

The morphologies of the as-synthesized samples are characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi) is carried out to investigate the elemental composition and chemical state. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Nanoscope Iva-Multimode) is used to characterize the thickness of 2D CPs NSs in tapping mode in air. The phase and crystal structures of the products were determined by Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD, Rigaku D/Max 2500PC) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) over a 2θ range of 10-80°. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 330 FTIR Spectromete) spectra are recorded in 4000–400 cm−1 region using KBr pellets. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 X) is used to characterize the work functions of 2D CP NSs and its composite nanomaterials. The fundamental electrical was performed using a Keysight B2910A precision source/measure unit with an incorporated direct current voltage supply. The interdigitated array electrode is composed of 10 pairs of ITO electrode digits (fingers) deposited onto a glass substrate with the following dimensions: 75 μm spacing, 5850 μm overlapping length, and 20 nm electrode thickness. Conductivity, σ, can be calculated by the following equation.
σ =
dI
2 n - 1
LhV
where d is the interelectrode spacing, I is the current, n is the number of electrode digits, L is the overlapping length of the electrodes, and h is electrode thickness (film deposition thickness).

In order to exert the gas sensing property of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs film, the structure of the sensor was designed as a planar rigid PCB substrate with a return-shaped planar Cu/Ni metal electrode. Such a component substrate structure can ensure that the gas sensing film attached thereto is uniform and controllable and can improve response of the film. Using micro-nano fabrication techniques: electrode patterns are visualized by sputtering Cu/Ni thin films, optical lithography techniques and etching techniques. The sensing films of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs were fabricated by spin coating technique (Spin coater: Laurell WS-650MZ-8NPPB) at 800 rpm for 30 s. Afterward, the Spin-coated samples were dried in the oven at 60 °C for 2 h.

In the NH3 gas sensing experimental, ambient air was used as the carrier gas and all ammonia sensing experimental measurements were taken at room temperature. The sensing properties of the sensors upon exposed to various concentrations of ammonia gas were investigated, which were achieved by injecting a certain volume of ammonia gas into a sealed container with a syringe. A data logger (Keysight 34470A) was used to record the corresponding resistance change of the sensors to investigate their sensing properties. The response (R), determined by R = Ra/Rg, was used for evaluating the sensing performances of sensors, where Ra and Rg were the measured resistance of the sensor in air and NH3 gas, respectively.

In order to obtain the 2D NSs, the growth of MOFs was limited in one of three dimensions via the combined role of surfactant (polyvinylpyrrolidon, (PVP)) and controlling agent (sodium citrate) [36,37]. The CQDs with average diameter of 2 nm were synthesized by a facile one-step pyrolytic synthesis method. The synthesized Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs were stirred at room temperature to obtain Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs with uniformly loaded CQDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images clearly reveal the Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs with about lateral size of 50 nm (Fig. 1 a and 1b) and thickness of ∼ 2.5 nm (Fig. 1c) proving the ultrathin nature of 2D Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs. In order to further confirm the successful synthesis of the material, elemental maps were recorded, and the results are shown in Fig. 1d. It can be seen that the four elements above are contained in the material, and there are no other elements except Co, Ni, C and N elements. The obtained Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs have a 2D ultrathin nanosheet morphology, and the morphology and phase structure can be maintained after incorporating CQDs onto the nanosheets’ surface. From Fig. 2 a, we can see that after the deposition of CQDs, the surface of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs becomes rough. TEM images show that the composite morphology of CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs exhibiting a regular sheet-like structure and modifying some small CQDs on its surface (Fig. 2b). Moreover, there is not notable change in the overall 2D ultrathin structure after adding CQDs. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows that CQDs (Fig. 2c) display uniform and dispersed spherical particles, and the size is about 2 nm. The individual carbon dot which possesses a crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.216 nm, which is coincide with corresponding to the lattice fringes of the (100) planes of graphite [38]. The atomic force microscope (AFM) image clearly confirms that the ultrathin structure of CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs is maintained, and the thickness is about 3 nm (Fig. 2d). The elemental maps of the obtained composite reveal Co, Ni, C and N element from CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs. All of the above results confirmed the successful preparation of ultrathin CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs composite.

As an important parameter of the gas sensor, the response/recovery time is defined as the time required for the sensor resistance to change by 90 %.Fig. 6 a and 6c indicates the response and recovery time curves of CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensors upon exposure to NH3 with various concentrations. The response-recovery time of the sensor does not change significantly along with the gas concentration change. The response time is approximately 40 s and recovery time is 10–20 s, indicating that the sensors exhibit excellent response recovery characteristics. In addition, the long-term stabilities of the two sensors were systematically evaluated (Fig. 6b and 6d). The sensor response did not show significant changes in repeated cycles at 5 day intervals, indicating that the sensor has good repeatability.

It is well known that changes in relative humidity (RH) can greatly affect the performance of room temperature sensors. Fig. 7 shows the ammonia sensing performance of sensors based on CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs (14.01 wt%) and pure Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs under various relative humidity (43–98 % RH). We have observed that the dependence of response value on relative humidity and gas concentration can be approximated by a linear relationship, which can be expressed in the following equation:
Re se p o n s e = α RH ( % ) +
β ( ppm )
+ Z 0
where α and β are two constant coefficients; Z0 is a constant term.

From the coefficients of the equation, it can be found that both the pure Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor and the CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor have a negative α value. This means that the responses of the sensors decrease as the humidity increases. Certainly, water molecules occupy any part of the active sites on the surface of sensor and interfere with the adsorption of target gas molecules at a higher humidity, which is the probable cause to decrease the sensor response [39,40]. In addition, we found that the absolute value of the α value of the CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor is smaller than that of the pure Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor, which means that the load of CQDs, to some extent, can improve the influence of humidity on pure Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor.

Although it is not yet possible to determine the specific mechanism of the gas sensing performance of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs, we have taken a series of characterizations to explore its underlying causes. It is known that upon exposure to reducing gas (NH3), the sensor resistance exhibited a pronounced decrement, which is the typical behaviour of an n-type semiconductor [37,38]. We speculate that this sensor is an n-type semiconductor, and electrons are the main charge carriers. When the sensor is exposed to the air, O2 is adsorbed on the surface to capture electrons from the conduction band. Because of the strong electronegativity of the oxygen atom, the captured free electrons can form chemisorbed oxygen O2 – (below 150 °C) causing the reduced conductivity [43,44]. When adsorbed oxygen ions having low binding energy and high oxidation capacity react with reducing gases such as NH3, the trapped electrons release to the conduction band and reduce the resistance. Above all, as observed from the PXRD spectrum, the crystal structure of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs remained unchanged before and after exposure to saturated NH3. This means that the NH3 molecule does not cause irreversible damage to the structure of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs, which makes it possible to detect ammonia gas potentially (Fig. 8 a and b).

In order to account for the high selectivity of sensors to NH3, the FTIR spectra of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs before and after adsorption of NH3 were compared. The changes of peaks at 1228, 1092, 1051 and 881 cm−1 and the red shift of NH3-M (M = Co, Ni) at (CN) were observed in Fig. 8c and d, respectively. These results suggest strong interactions between NH3 and the framework of Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs, and the red shift phenomenon means a stronger interaction between the NH3 molecules and the CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs backbone. In addition, the appearance of bimodal also provides theoretical support for the selective adsorption of NH3 to metal sites.

Meanwhile, UPS (ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy) characterization can provide us with the work function of the materials. Comparing the UPS results before and after the adsorption of ammonia, we found that: the work function of the Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs decreased by 0.72 eV, 0.96 eV, respectively (Fig. 8e and f). Generally, when the surface of materials has adsorbate or is modified, if the sample obtains electrons from the adsorbate, the work function decreases, conversely, the work function increases [45]. It is well known that ammonia loses electrons easily. The recombination between the electron in Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs film and the doped electron from ammonia increases carrier concentration and decreases the resistance of the sensor, which is consistent with our experimental phenomena. Notably, the change in work function of CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs after adsorption of ammonia is greater than Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs, which is most likely due to the presence of intense electron-hole activity between CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and NH3 molecules. This also explains why CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor has a higher response value for NH3 detection. As we known, CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs are CPs materials with the orderly structure and adjustable pores on the surface. Providing high accessible surface area and small steric hindrance, it is easy for the gas molecules effectively inlet and outflow. All of these contribute to the high performances of our sensors.

In summary, we demonstrate a chemical resistance ultrasensitive ammonia (NH3) sensor via the route of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated on the 2D ultrathin CPs NSs. The performance of the Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs and CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs as a sensing layer on a resistance sensor for NH3 detection at room temperature was successfully demonstrated. The synthesized CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs compound materials exhibited high response NH3 response (Ra/Rg = 87.7 to 30 ppm). Moreover, CQDs@Co[Ni(CN)4] NSs sensor exhibited good linear fit curve, short response/recovery time and excellent repeatability. This investigation not only develops a synthesis procedure of states with 2D CPs ultrathin structure, but also provides a simple and feasible strategy for improving gas sensor property of CQDs doped CPs nanomaterials. This work provides an effective way to construct new electrode materials for high-performance gas sensor devices via the rational design.

Dongzhi Zhang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Supervision. Yuwei Luo: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhaodi Huang: . Mingcong Tang: Investigation. Jianpeng Sun: Investigation. Xiaokang Wang: Investigation. Xia Wang: Investigation. Yutong Wang: Investigation. Wenting Wu: Investigation. Fangna Dai: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51777215, 21771191), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (19CX05001A).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional latex/polytetrafluoroethylene-based triboelectric nanogenerator for self-powered organ-like MXene/metal-organic framework-derived CuO nanohybrid ammonia sensor
ACS Nano.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Y. Wang, D. Z. Zhang, Y. Yang, Q. Mi, J. H. Zhang, L. D. Yu, Multifunctional latex/polytetrafluoroethylene-based triboelectric nanogenerator for self-powered organ-like MXene/metal-organic framework-derived CuO nanohybrid ammonia sensor, ACS Nano. 15 (2021) 2911-2919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a cloud-based epidermal MoSe2 device for hazardous gas sensing
Adv. Funct. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UV illumination-enhanced ultrasensitive ammonia gas sensor based on (001)TiO2/MXene heterostructure for food spoilage detection
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Z. Zhang, S. J. Yu, X. W. Wang, J. K. Huang, W. J. Pan, J. H. Zhang, J. B. Zeng, UV illumination-enhanced ultrasensitive ammonia gas sensor based on (001)TiO2/MXene heterostructure for food spoilage detection, J. Hazard. Mater. 423 (2022) 127160.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. H. Zhang, J. Y. Wang, N. L. Torad, W. Xia, M. A. Aslam, Y. V. Kaneti, Z. F. Hou, Z. J. Ding, B. Da, A. Fatehmulla, A.M. Aldhafiri, .W.A. Farooq, J. Tang, Y. Bando, Y. Yamauchi, Rational design of nanoporous MoS2/VS2 heteroarchitecture for ultrahigh performance ammonia sensors, Small. 16 (2020) 1901718.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A rapid-response room-temperature planar type gas sensor based on DPA-Ph-DBPzDCN for the sensitive detection of NH3
J. Mater. Chem. A.

Lu, A rapid-response room-temperature planar type gas sensor based on DPA-Ph-DBPzDCN for the sensitive detection of NH3, J. Mater. Chem. A. 7 (2019) 4744-4750.
